content:
# Joshua Chapter 9 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), folio pages 232-233 | Codex Vaticanus (LXX)
**Source Image:** `JOS_009_page_001.jpg`, `JOS_009_page_002.jpg`

---

**9:1** And it came to pass, when all the kings who were beyond the Jordan heard — in the hill country and in the lowland and on all the coast of the Great Sea toward Lebanon: the Hittite and the Amorite, the Canaanite, the Perizzite, the Hivite, and the Jebusite —

**9:2** they gathered themselves together to fight against Joshua and against Israel, with one mouth.

**9:3** But the inhabitants of Gibeon heard what Joshua had done to Jericho and to Ai,

**9:4** and they also acted with cunning. And they went and disguised themselves as envoys, and they took worn-out sacks upon their donkeys, and wineskins, worn-out and torn and bound up,

**9:5** and worn-out and patched sandals upon their feet, and worn-out garments upon them; and all the bread of their provision was dry — it had become crumbs.

**9:6** And they went to Joshua, to the camp at Gilgal, and they said to him and to the men of Israel, "From a far land we have come; and now, cut for us a covenant."

**9:7** And the men of Israel said to the Hivite, "Perhaps you are dwelling in our midst; and how shall we cut for you a covenant?"

**9:8** And they said to Joshua, "We are your servants." And Joshua said to them, "Who are you, and from where do you come?"

**9:9** And they said to him, "From a very far land your servants have come, on account of the name of YHWH your God, for we have heard his fame, and all that he did in Egypt,

**9:10** and all that he did to the two kings of the Amorites who were beyond the Jordan — to Sihon king of Heshbon, and to Og king of Bashan, who was in Ashtaroth.

**9:11** And our elders and all the inhabitants of our land said to us, saying, 'Take provision in your hand for the journey, and go to meet them, and say to them, "We are your servants; and now, cut for us a covenant."'

**9:12** This our bread — hot we provisioned ourselves with it from our houses on the day of our going out to come to you; and now, behold, it is dry and has become crumbs.

**9:13** And these wineskins of wine which we filled were new, and behold, they are torn. And these our garments and our sandals are worn out from the very great journey."

**9:14** And the men took some of their provision, but the mouth of YHWH they did not ask.

**9:15** And Joshua made peace with them and cut for them a covenant to let them live, and the leaders of the congregation swore an oath to them.

**9:16** And it came to pass at the end of three days, after they had cut for them a covenant, that they heard that they were near to them, and that in their midst they were dwelling.

**9:17** And the Sons of Israel set out and came to their cities on the third day. And their cities were Gibeon, and Chephirah, and Beeroth, and Kiriath-jearim.

**9:18** And the Sons of Israel did not strike them, for the leaders of the congregation had sworn to them by YHWH, the God of Israel. And all the congregation murmured against the leaders.

**9:19** And all the leaders said to all the congregation, "We have sworn to them by YHWH, the God of Israel, and now we are not able to touch them.

**9:20** This we shall do to them: we shall let them live, so that there shall not be wrath upon us on account of the oath which we swore to them."

**9:21** And the leaders said to them, "Let them live." And they became cutters of wood and drawers of water for all the congregation, as the leaders had spoken to them.

**9:22** And Joshua summoned them and spoke to them, saying, "Why did you deceive us, saying, 'We are very far from you,' when you are dwelling in our midst?

**9:23** And now, cursed are you, and there shall never be cut off from you a servant — and cutters of wood and drawers of water for the house of my God."

**9:24** And they answered Joshua and said, "Because it was surely told to your servants that YHWH your God commanded Moses his servant to give to you all the land, and to destroy all the inhabitants of the land from before you; and we feared greatly for our lives because of you, and we did this thing.

**9:25** And now, behold, we are in your hand. As it is good and as it is right in your eyes to do to us, do."

**9:26** And he did so to them, and he delivered them from the hand of the Sons of Israel, and they did not kill them.

**9:27** And Joshua made them on that day cutters of wood and drawers of water for the congregation and for the altar of YHWH, to this day, in the place which he would choose.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Method
Each verse was compared across all three translations (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) and checked against the manuscript images of Leningrad Codex folio pages 232-233 and Codex Vaticanus folio 168. Where all three translators agreed on a reading, that agreement was adopted. Where they diverged, the manuscript was consulted and a synthesis was chosen favoring accuracy to the Hebrew while maintaining readable English.

### Broad Consensus
Joshua 9 produced strong agreement across all three translators on the following points:
- **YHWH** retained as the divine name throughout (vv. 9, 14, 18, 19, 24, 27).
- **Sons of Israel** retained for bene yisra'el (vv. 17, 18, 26).
- **"Cut a covenant"** (karat berit) preserved in its literal Hebrew idiom rather than flattened to "make a treaty" (vv. 6, 7, 11, 15, 16). PNEUMA used "made a treaty" — overruled in favor of the literal idiom per SOPHIA rules.
- **"Cutters of wood and drawers of water"** as the recurring merism for menial labor (vv. 21, 23, 27). EMET used "choppers," LOGOS and PNEUMA used "cutters"/"woodcutters." Consensus: "cutters of wood" — closer to the Hebrew khoteve etsim and avoiding the more informal "choppers."
- All three correctly identified the six-nation list in v. 1 (Hittite, Amorite, Canaanite, Perizzite, Hivite, Jebusite), matching the Leningrad Codex.
- All three correctly noted the Deuteronomic formula in v. 27 ("the place which he would choose").

### Major Decisions

**1. Verse 2 — "with one mouth" vs. "with one accord" vs. "united as one"**
EMET preserved the literal "one mouth" (peh echad). LOGOS rendered "with one accord." PNEUMA rendered "united as one." SOPHIA adopts "with one mouth" — the body-part idiom is vivid, intelligible in English, and preserves a Hebrew metaphor that appears elsewhere in the text (cf. v. 14, "mouth of YHWH"). Retaining both "mouth" usages allows the reader to feel the thematic wordplay: the kings have "one mouth," but Israel fails to consult "the mouth of YHWH." See Disagreement Log.

**2. Verse 4 — vayyitstayyaru: "disguised themselves" vs. "disguised themselves as envoys" vs. "provisioned themselves"**
All three translators follow the MT consonantal text with the "envoy/disguise" reading over the LXX's episitisanto ("provisioned themselves"). EMET rendered "disguised themselves," LOGOS and PNEUMA rendered "disguised themselves as envoys." SOPHIA adopts "disguised themselves as envoys" — the Hitpael of tsir ("envoy") most naturally yields "made themselves out as envoys," and this reading fits the narrative context where the deception is the central action. The LXX variant is noted but not followed.

**3. Verse 5 — niqqudim: "crumbs" vs. "crumbled" vs. "crumbling to pieces"**
EMET rendered "had become crumbs," LOGOS rendered "had become crumbled," PNEUMA rendered "crumbling to pieces." The Hebrew niqqudim literally means "dotted/spotted" — referring to bread that has dried and broken apart, possibly spotted with mold. SOPHIA adopts "had become crumbs" — a concise nominal rendering that preserves the stative quality of the Hebrew without over-interpreting. See Disagreement Log.

**4. Verse 6 — Word order: "From a far land we have come" vs. "We have come from a far country"**
EMET preserved the Hebrew fronted word order ("From a land far we have come"). LOGOS and PNEUMA moved to standard English order. SOPHIA preserves the Hebrew fronting: "From a far land we have come" — the displacement emphasizes "far," which is precisely the Gibeonites' deceptive point. The Hebrew conjunction style is preserved with "and now."

**5. Verse 7 — "the Hivite" (singular collective) vs. "the Hivites" (plural)**
EMET preserved the singular collective "the Hivite" (ha-Khivvi). LOGOS rendered "the Hivites" (plural). PNEUMA rendered "the Hivites." The Leningrad Codex reads the singular collective, which is typical of Hebrew ethnonym usage. SOPHIA adopts the singular "the Hivite" per the MT. See Disagreement Log.

**6. Verse 12 — Structure and phrasing**
EMET preserved the full Hebrew clause structure: "hot we provisioned ourselves with it from our houses on the day of our going out to go to you." LOGOS rendered more smoothly: "we took it hot as our provision." PNEUMA rendered freely: "it was hot from the oven when we packed it." SOPHIA follows EMET's structure closely, retaining the participial "on the day of our going out" and the fronted "hot" — the Gibeonites are performing their lie with rhetorical emphasis.

**7. Verse 14 — "the mouth of YHWH they did not ask" vs. "did not ask at the mouth of YHWH"**
EMET: "the mouth of YHWH they did not ask." LOGOS: "they did not ask at the mouth of YHWH." PNEUMA: "they did not ask YHWH." SOPHIA adopts EMET's word order, which preserves the Hebrew fronting of et pi YHWH — the "mouth of YHWH" is placed first for emphasis, mirroring the Hebrew syntax. PNEUMA's omission of "mouth" loses a critical image that connects to v. 2 ("one mouth") and to the broader theology of divine oracle (cf. Num 27:21). See Disagreement Log.

**8. Verse 23 — "shall never be cut off from you a servant"**
EMET: "shall never be cut off from you a slave." LOGOS: "none of you shall be cut off from being a servant." PNEUMA: "You will never stop being servants." The Hebrew reads ve-lo' yikkaret mikkem eved — literally "and not shall-be-cut-off from-you a servant." SOPHIA renders: "there shall never be cut off from you a servant" — preserving the niph'al passive and the sense that servitude will never depart from them. "Servant" preferred over "slave" (with LOGOS) as eved carries a broader range. See Disagreement Log.

**9. Verse 24 — Infinitive absolute: hagged huggad**
EMET: "telling it was told." LOGOS: "it was surely told." PNEUMA: "we were clearly told." The infinitive absolute construction hagged huggad (Hiphil infinitive absolute + Hophal finite verb) demands the doubling per SOPHIA rules. SOPHIA adopts "it was surely told" — the adverb "surely" captures the emphatic force of the infinitive absolute in natural English. EMET's "telling it was told" is more literally doubled but reads awkwardly. The emphasis is preserved. See Disagreement Log.

**10. Verse 27 — "he would choose" vs. "YHWH would choose"**
EMET and LOGOS read "he would choose" (matching the MT yivkhar with implied subject). PNEUMA rendered "YHWH would choose" (making the subject explicit). SOPHIA follows the MT with "he would choose" — the implied subject is YHWH, and the Hebrew does not name him explicitly here. The Deuteronomic formula is preserved in its understated form.

### Textual Notes

- **V. 1 — "beyond the Jordan"** (be-ever ha-Yarden): This is perspectival. In context it refers to the western side of the Jordan — the kings within Canaan — as indicated by the geographic catalogue (hill country, lowland, coast). The Hebrew ever can mean either bank depending on the narrator's standpoint.
- **V. 4 — Ketiv/Qere**: The Leningrad Codex preserves a notable Ketiv/Qere at vayyitstayyaru. The Ketiv supports the tsir ("envoy") root; the Qere may reflect a scribal tradition favoring the tsedah ("provision") reading. The LXX Vaticanus supports "provisioned" (episitisanto). SOPHIA follows the MT consonantal text.
- **V. 7 — Dramatic irony**: The narrator identifies the visitors as "the Hivite" — a name the Gibeonites never gave. The Hivites are among the nations designated for destruction (Deut 7:1). Israel suspects the truth but proceeds without consulting YHWH.
- **V. 9-10 — Selective omission**: The Gibeonites carefully cite Egypt, Sihon, and Og — events from before the Jordan crossing — while omitting Jericho and Ai. A distant people would know old reports but not recent local events. This is deliberate narrative craft.
- **V. 14 — The theological pivot**: "The mouth of YHWH they did not ask" is the structural hinge of the chapter. The phrase implies inquiry through the Urim and Thummim or prophetic oracle (cf. Num 27:21). Everything before this verse builds the deception; everything after reveals its consequences.
- **V. 17 — Four Gibeonite cities**: Gibeon (modern el-Jib), Chephirah (Tell Kefirah), Beeroth (possibly el-Bireh), and Kiriath-jearim (Tell el-Azhar). All are in the Benjamin hill country, approximately six to eight miles northwest of Jerusalem. These cities later fall within Benjamin's allotment (Josh 18:25-28).
- **V. 18 — "murmured"** (vayyillonu): The same verb (lun) used for Israel's wilderness complaints against Moses (Exod 15:24; 16:2; Num 14:2). The echo is deliberate — communal dissent is a recurring pattern.
- **V. 23 — Echoes of Genesis 9:25**: Joshua's curse ("cursed are you") echoes the curse of Canaan (arur Kena'an eved avadim). The Gibeonites — who are Hivites, who are Canaanites — receive a diminished version of that ancient curse. Yet their servitude is directed to "the house of my God," giving it a sacred rather than chattel character. Their descendants appear as the Nethinim ("the given ones") in Ezra 2:43-58 and Nehemiah 7:46-60.
- **V. 27 — "the place which he would choose"**: The Deuteronomic formula (ha-maqom asher yivkhar; cf. Deut 12:5, 11, 14). The imperfect yivkhar points forward to a sanctuary site not yet revealed. Jerusalem is unnamed. The Gibeonites' servitude is linked to YHWH's future chosen ground.

### LXX Divergences (Vaticanus, folio 168)
The LXX of Joshua 9 diverges significantly from the MT in verse numbering, arrangement, and some content. Notable differences:
- V. 2: The LXX adds explicit reference to the region of "Gai" (the Ai).
- V. 4: The LXX reads episitisanto ("they provisioned themselves") rather than the MT's "disguised themselves as envoys," reflecting either a different Vorlage or interpretive tradition.
- The LXX rearranges some material and adjusts verse divisions relative to the MT.
- SOPHIA follows the Masoretic text of the Leningrad Codex as the primary source throughout.

---

## Disagreement Log

### 9:2 — "with one mouth" vs. "with one accord" vs. "united as one"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "with one mouth" | Literal body-part idiom preserved |
| LOGOS | "with one accord" | English idiom for unanimity |
| PNEUMA | "united as one" | Dynamic equivalent for peh echad |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "with one mouth" (EMET). The idiom is intelligible in English and preserves a thematic connection to v. 14 ("the mouth of YHWH"). Flattening to "one accord" or "united as one" loses this verbal echo.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET. Not theologically significant — a matter of literary texture.

---

### 9:4 — "disguised themselves" vs. "disguised themselves as envoys"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "disguised themselves" | General deception; avoids specifying the debated tsir |
| LOGOS | "disguised themselves as envoys" | Follows tsir root; makes the ambassadorial disguise explicit |
| PNEUMA | "disguised as envoys on a long journey" | Dynamic expansion with added context |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "disguised themselves as envoys" (LOGOS). The Hitpael of tsir supports this reading, and it fits the narrative: the Gibeonites present themselves as distant ambassadors, not merely as disguised travelers. PNEUMA's added "on a long journey" is interpretive expansion.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Lexically significant — the rare Hitpael form is debated.

---

### 9:5 — niqqudim: "crumbs" vs. "crumbled" vs. "crumbling to pieces"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "had become crumbs" | Nominal; preserves stative sense |
| LOGOS | "had become crumbled" | Verbal/adjectival rendering |
| PNEUMA | "crumbling to pieces" | Dynamic; emphasizes ongoing deterioration |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "had become crumbs" (EMET). The Hebrew niqqudim is a nominal form ("dotted/spotted things"), and the stative "had become crumbs" best reflects the completed state described.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET. Not theologically significant.

---

### 9:6 — Word order: fronted vs. standard English

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "From a land far we have come" | Hebrew word order preserved |
| LOGOS | "We have come from a far country" | Standard English word order |
| PNEUMA | "We have come from a distant country" | Standard English word order |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted fronted order: "From a far land we have come" (adapted from EMET). The fronting of the prepositional phrase is deliberate in the Hebrew — it emphasizes distance, which is the core of the Gibeonites' lie. Smoothing to English order weakens the rhetorical emphasis.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET (with minor smoothing). A matter of translation philosophy.

---

### 9:7 — "the Hivite" (singular) vs. "the Hivites" (plural)

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the Hivite" | Singular collective per MT |
| LOGOS | "the Hivites" | Plural for English clarity |
| PNEUMA | "the Hivites" | Plural for English clarity |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the Hivite" (EMET). The Leningrad Codex reads the singular collective ha-Khivvi, which is the standard Hebrew ethnonym form. Preserving it maintains the texture of the Hebrew.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET. Not theologically significant.

---

### 9:14 — "the mouth of YHWH they did not ask" vs. "they did not ask at the mouth of YHWH" vs. "they did not ask YHWH"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the mouth of YHWH they did not ask" | Preserves Hebrew word order and "mouth" image |
| LOGOS | "they did not ask at the mouth of YHWH" | Retains "mouth" but uses English word order |
| PNEUMA | "they did not ask YHWH" | Omits "mouth" for simplicity |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "the mouth of YHWH they did not ask" (EMET). This is the theological pivot of the chapter and must not be softened. The fronted object (et pi YHWH) mirrors the Hebrew emphasis. The word "mouth" connects to v. 2 ("one mouth") and to the concept of divine oracle. PNEUMA's omission of "mouth" loses a critical theological image.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET. Theologically significant — the "mouth of YHWH" is the chapter's central theological concept.

---

### 9:15 — "cut a covenant" vs. "made a treaty"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "cut for them a covenant" | Literal Hebrew idiom preserved |
| LOGOS | "cut a covenant with them" | Literal Hebrew idiom preserved |
| PNEUMA | "made a treaty with them" | Dynamic; modern English equivalent |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "cut for them a covenant" (EMET/LOGOS hybrid). The Hebrew karat berit ("cut a covenant") is a foundational biblical idiom referring to the ritual of cutting an animal and passing between the halves (cf. Gen 15:10, 17). Per SOPHIA rules, Hebrew idioms with theological weight are preserved. PNEUMA's "made a treaty" loses the covenantal cutting imagery.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of EMET/LOGOS. Theologically significant — covenant-cutting is a core biblical concept.

---

### 9:23 — Rendering of the perpetual servitude clause

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "shall never be cut off from you a slave" | Literal; uses "slave" for eved |
| LOGOS | "none of you shall be cut off from being a servant" | Reframes the passive construction |
| PNEUMA | "You will never stop being servants" | Dynamic paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "there shall never be cut off from you a servant" (adapted from EMET with "servant" from LOGOS). The niph'al yikkaret preserves the passive voice — servitude will not depart from them. "Servant" (over "slave") better represents the broader semantic range of eved, especially since the servitude is directed to "the house of my God."

**Status:** Resolved as EMET/LOGOS hybrid. Lexically significant — eved ranges from "servant" to "slave."

---

### 9:24 — Infinitive absolute: hagged huggad

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "telling it was told" | Doubling preserved literally |
| LOGOS | "it was surely told" | Emphatic adverb captures force |
| PNEUMA | "we were clearly told" | Dynamic; active voice with adverb |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "it was surely told" (LOGOS). The infinitive absolute hagged + finite huggad demands emphasis, which "surely" provides clearly. EMET's "telling it was told" is more literally doubled but reads unnaturally. PNEUMA's shift to active voice ("we were clearly told") changes the grammatical structure of the Hophal passive. The emphatic adverb "surely" is the standard convention for rendering the infinitive absolute in formal English translation.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. A matter of rendering the infinitive absolute — per SOPHIA rules, doubling should be preserved, and "surely" accomplishes this idiomatically.

---

### 9:21 — "choppers" vs. "cutters" vs. "woodcutters"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "choppers of wood" | Active participle; vigorous English |
| LOGOS | "cutters of wood" | Active participle; closer to khotevei |
| PNEUMA | "woodcutters" | Compound noun; modern English |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted "cutters of wood" (LOGOS). The Hebrew khotevei etsim uses the construct chain (participle + noun), which "cutters of wood" mirrors precisely. "Choppers" is slightly informal; "woodcutters" as a compound noun loses the construct chain structure.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Not theologically significant.
